title: Iterating Collections 🔄
description: Use the each block to loop over massive Lists and Hashes effortlessly.

slides.title: Data Digestion 🍽️
slides.content:
Remember the `.each` iterator we learned about? It works perfectly with ranges (`1..5`).

But the absolute best use of `.each` is traversing Arrays and Hashes!

slides.title: Iterating an Array
slides.content: Run this! `.each` steps through the array sequentially, handing the exact list item over to the parameter `|name|` one by one.
slides.codeSnippet:
friends = ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"]

friends.each do |name|
  puts "Hello there, #{name}!"
end

quizData.question: When iterating over an Array using `.each`, what gets passed to the block parameter `|x|` on every cycle?

options.text: The actual value stored in that list position.

quizData.explanation: With an array, the `.each` iterator grabs the raw item itself (`"Bob"`) and feeds it straight to your code block.

slides.title: Iterating a Hash 🗝️
slides.content:
Hashes aren't single items, they are Key-Value *pairs*!

As a result, when you call `.each` on a Hash, the block expects you to catch BOTH pieces of data!

`hash.each do |key, value|`

slides.title: Hash Iteration
slides.content: Watch how we cleanly grab both the Key (the country) and the Value (the capital) simultaneously during every loop cycle.
slides.codeSnippet:
capitals = { 
  usa: "Washington D.C.", 
  france: "Paris", 
  japan: "Tokyo"
}

capitals.each do |country, capital|
  puts "The capital of #{country} is #{capital}!"
end

quizData.question: Why do we use two parameters `|key, value|` when iterating a Hash?

options.text: Because it runs twice as fast.
Because Hashes always hand you the Key and its attached Value simultaneously.

quizData.explanation: You're grabbing a pair, so you must catch the pair! The first parameter is always the Key, the second is always the Value.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: You can catch hash iteration with a single parameter `|item|`. If you do this, `item` will be a 2-element Array containing `[key, value]`. However, catching them as two separate named variables `|k, v|` is almost always preferred!
